1.pericope_label: From Narrow Straits to Spacious Victory
1.synthesis: Psalm 118 is the climactic Hallel psalm, the liturgical apex of the Egyptian Hallel collection. Its opening movement (vv. 1–4) is antiphonal, calling three groups to declare YHWH’s hesed: the house of Israel, the house of Aaron, and those who fear the Lord. The individual narrative of deliverance begins at verse 5: “Out of my distress I called on the Lord; the Lord answered me and set me free.” The Hebrew is charged with spatial imagery: min-hammetsar (from the narrow place, the strait) to merhav (a wide and spacious place). The confident declaration follows: “The Lord is on my side; I will not fear. What can man do to me?” (v. 6, quoted in Heb 13:6). The triple encirclement by nations (vv. 10–12) is defeated in the same way each time: “in the name of the Lord I cut them off.” The Song of the Sea is cited verbatim: “The Lord is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation” (v. 14; cf. Exod 15:2), identifying the psalmist’s deliverance with Israel’s Exodus. The right hand of the Lord is exalted three times in verses 15–16, and the psalmist affirms: “I shall not die, but I shall live, and recount the deeds of the Lord” (v. 17).
1.historical_context: Psalm 118’s liturgical setting is the entrance processional to the Jerusalem temple, as evidenced by the antiphonal structure of verses 1–4 (different groups responding) and the gate-entry dialogue of verses 19–20. The citation of Exodus 15:2 (v. 14) connects every subsequent deliverance to the paradigmatic Exodus rescue, making Psalm 118 a liturgy of re-enactment: every personal rescue is a recapitulation of the Exodus. Hebrews 13:6 quotes verse 6 to ground the Christian community’s confidence in the face of persecution. The triple encirclement (vv. 10–12) and the threefold declaration of victory “in the name of the Lord” reflects the ANE convention of describing complete or repeated attacks.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 118:1–18 divides into the liturgical frame (vv. 1–4), the individual narrative (vv. 5–18), with the narrative itself structured around three key confessions: “YHWH is on my side” (vv. 6–7), the triple defeat of encircling enemies (vv. 10–12), and the tripling of “the right hand of the Lord is exalted” (vv. 15–16). The Exodus citation (v. 14) appears at the structural center of the narrative section, grounding the individual story in the foundational national deliverance. The declaration of not dying but living (v. 17) prepares for the entrance into the sanctuary gates (v. 19).
1.theological_application: The spatial metaphor of min-hammetsar to merhav—from the constricting strait to the spacious place (v. 5)—is the Psalter’s foundational metaphor for salvation. YHWH’s rescues always move from narrowness and constraint toward space, freedom, and expansiveness. The triple refrain “in the name of the Lord I cut them off” (vv. 10–12) is not magical invocation but the declaration that YHWH’s name—his covenant character and presence—is the operative power in every battle. The antiphonal opening models congregational worship as the proper frame for every individual story of rescue.
1.christology: Jesus’s entry into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday was a deliberate staging of Psalm 118: the crowds cried “Hosanna!” (v. 25) and “Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord!” (v. 26), placing Jesus within the psalm’s framework as the one who comes in YHWH’s name toward the sanctuary. The right hand of the Lord exalted (vv. 15–16) is fulfilled in the resurrection and ascension: Christ’s declaration “I shall not die but live” (v. 17) becomes the interpretive key to Easter morning, the supreme instance of YHWH’s bringing his servant from min-hammetsar to merhav.

19.pericope_label: The Rejected Stone and the Day the Lord Has Made
19.synthesis: Psalm 118’s second section opens with an entrance liturgy at the gates: “Open to me the gates of righteousness, that I may enter through them and give thanks to the Lord” (v. 19). The response: “This is the gate of the Lord; the righteous shall enter through it” (v. 20). Then comes the psalm’s most quoted verse in the New Testament: “The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone (rosh pinnah); this is the Lord’s doing; it is marvelous in our eyes” (vv. 22–23). This is immediately followed by the most famous of all the Hallel declarations: “This is the day that the Lord has made; let us rejoice and be glad in it” (v. 24). The petition “Save us, we pray, O Lord!” (hoshi’a-na, v. 25) gives us the word “Hosanna.” The blessing “Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord!” follows (v. 26). The psalm closes with the inclusio that closes the entire Hallel: “Give thanks to the Lord, for he is good; for his steadfast love endures forever” (v. 29), mirroring verse 1.
19.historical_context: The cornerstone image (v. 22) derives from the practice of selecting the foundational corner-stone (rosh pinnah, “head of the corner”) of a building—the capstone that locked together two walls and gave the structure its alignment. The image of a builder rejecting this stone (finding it unsuitable) only to have it installed as the structure’s most crucial element is the reversal pattern of the psalm applied architecturally. No other psalm verse in the New Testament is quoted more times across more books: Matthew 21:42, Mark 12:10, Luke 20:17, Acts 4:11, 1 Peter 2:7, and by allusion in Ephesians 2:20.
19.literary_structure: Psalm 118:19–29 forms the sanctuary-entry and theological climax of the Hallel. The entrance dialogue (vv. 19–20) gives way to thanksgiving (v. 21), the cornerstone oracle (vv. 22–24), the petition and blessing (vv. 25–27), individual thanksgiving (v. 28), and the closing doxological inclusio (v. 29 = v. 1). The cornerstone reversal sits at the structural heart of this section: the rejected-then-exalted pattern mirrors the movement from the narrow straits to the spacious place in vv. 5–18, but now applied not to a battle victory but to a construction event. The “this is the Lord’s doing” (v. 23) names the agent of every reversal in the psalm.
19.theological_application: The cornerstone metaphor offers a hermeneutical principle: what human builders reject as unsuitable, YHWH installs as the keystone of his entire structure. The community that sings Psalm 118 is invited to view its own experiences of rejection, marginalization, and apparent uselessness through this lens: what the world dismisses as waste material, YHWH may be preparing for a structural role in his purposes. “This is the Lord’s doing; it is marvelous in our eyes” (v. 23) is the confessional response to every such reversal, acknowledging that the architectural logic is YHWH’s, not human builders’.
19.christology: The New Testament’s saturation with Psalm 118:22 is the most concentrated application of a single Old Testament verse to Christ across all four Gospels and multiple Epistles. Jesus applies it to himself in his parable of the wicked tenants (Matt 21:42); Peter applies it to the resurrection in Acts 4:11 and to the church in 1 Pet 2:7. “This is the day the Lord has made” (v. 24) is the day of resurrection: the day when the rejected stone was installed as head of the corner by the Father’s vindication. The Hosanna of verse 25 and the “blessed is he who comes” of verse 26 are the exact liturgical words with which the crowd received Jesus on Palm Sunday—singing the psalm they were living.
